channel.title: Nature Materials
channel.description: Nature Materials is a multidisciplinary journal aimed at bringing together cutting-edge research across the entire spectrum of materials science and technology. Nature Materials covers all applied and fundamental aspects of the synthesis/processing, structure/composition, properties and performance of materials. Nature Materials provides a forum for the development of a common identity among materials scientists while encouraging researchers to cross established subdisciplinary lines. To achieve this, Nature Materials takes an interdisciplinary, integrated and balanced approach to all areas of materials research while fostering the exchange of ideas between scientists involved in different communities.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Linking metal oxidation to oxygen reactivity
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 17 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02540-7

Using multiple spectroscopies, it is shown that water oxidation on iridium oxides is driven by accumulated oxygen ligand holes.
item.dc:title: Linking metal oxidation to oxygen reactivity
item.dc:creator: A. R. Muñoz
T. E. Jones

item.title: Conventional wisdom about threshold switches challenged
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 17 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02543-4

Nanoscaled selectors made of elemental selenium that show picoampere-level leakage current and an on/off ratio of 108 call for rethinking electrical transport mechanisms.
item.dc:title: Conventional wisdom about threshold switches challenged
item.dc:creator: Nils Holle
Martin Salinga

item.title: Gold-activated persulfate p-doping of organic semiconductors
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 17 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02547-0

Achieving lateral doping gradients in organic semiconductors (OSCs) via solution processing is crucial but remains a challenge. A gold-activated persulfate doping strategy can locally oxidize OSCs and create a lateral doping gradient, enabling low contact resistance and high carrier mobility in solution-processed organic field-effect transistors.
item.dc:title: Gold-activated persulfate p-doping of organic semiconductors
item.dc:creator: Tiefeng Liu
Matilde Silveri
Zesheng Liu
Sang Young Jeong
Qiao He
Giannis G. Gkikas
Wenlong Jin
Chi-Yuan Yang
Tom P. A. van der Pol
Feng Zhang
Christina Kousseff
Anna Martinelli
Iain McCulloch
Martin Heeney
Han Young Woo
Alessandro Motta
Mats Fahlman
Simone Fabiano

item.title: A nanoporous capacitive electrochemical ratchet for continuous ion separations
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 13 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02511-y

An ion pump that does not require redox reactions to drive aqueous ions against forces is demonstrated, and may find applications in selective ion separation and water desalination.
item.dc:title: A nanoporous capacitive electrochemical ratchet for continuous ion separations
item.dc:creator: Rylan Kautz
Alon Herman
Ethan J. Heffernan
Keren Shushan Alshochat
Eden Grossman
Rahul Saxena
Camila Muñetón
David Larson
Joel W. Ager III
Francesca M. Toma
Shane Ardo
Gideon Segev

item.title: Grain boundary stabilization of fluorite ferroelectrics
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 13 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02533-6

Electron microscopy and spectroscopy, together with electronic structure calculations, are used to show that orbital ordering of ions at grain boundaries can facilitate the stabilization of ferroelectric phases.
item.dc:title: Grain boundary stabilization of fluorite ferroelectrics
item.dc:creator: Shiyu Wang
Hai Zhong
Siyi Song
Ang Gao
Qinghua Zhang
Dong Su
Kuijuan Jin
Chen Ge
Lin Gu

item.title: Pathways to commercially viable organic photovoltaic materials
item.content:encoded:
Nature Materials, Published online: 13 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02501-0

Low-cost, efficient and stable materials are required to enable the widespread application of organic photovoltaics. In this Review, the challenges associated with designing low-cost materials, developing eco-friendly solvents and understanding intrinsic degradation mechanisms of organic photovoltaics are discussed.
item.dc:title: Pathways to commercially viable organic photovoltaic materials
item.dc:creator: Jianhua Han
Han Xu
Daniel Corzo
Anirudh Sharma
Derya Baran
